Title: Cadaverine regulates biotin synthesis to modulate primary root growth in Arabidopsis

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Biotin carboxyl carrier protein subunit of chloroplastic heteromeric acetyl-CoA carboxylase complex
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: No significant transcriptional change with cadaverine treatment in seedlings
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in seedling roots and shoots; biotinylated protein decreases after cadaverine treatment, especially in roots
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Energy storage lipids contributing to plant carbon and energy reserves
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Whole seedlings; TAG profile measured in seedlings
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Cadaverine reduces BCCP1 biotinylation without significantly changing total BCCP1 protein abundance, consistent with reduced ACCase activity and lower TAG accumulation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BCCP1 is a biotinylated target protein affected by cadaverine treatment. 2. Cadaverine decreased biotinylated BCCP1 in both roots and shoots, with stronger reduction in roots. 3. Total BCCP1 protein abundance and transcript level were not significantly altered, indicating reduced biotin availability rather than reduced gene expression. 4. Reduced BCCP1 biotinylation is linked to decreased fatty acid synthesis and TAG accumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Western blot with streptavidin probe; western blot with BCCP1-specific antibody; qRT-PCR; lipid profiling by tandem MS
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Lipidomics by tandem mass spectrometry; accession number not provided
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced biotinylation likely decreases ACCase activity and fatty acid biosynthesis, lowering TAG content
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper interprets BCCP1 as a downstream biotin-dependent effector rather than the primary cadaverine target

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: No significant transcriptional change with cadaverine treatment in seedlings
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in roots and shoots; total protein increased in cdh3 roots after cadaverine treatment while biotinylated fraction did not increase
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports amino acid catabolism/homeostasis and central metabolism
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Serves as a biotinylated reporter protein; cadaverine had little effect on biotinylated MCCA overall, but cdh3 roots accumulated more MCCA apoprotein under treatment
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MCCA biotinylation was monitored to assess effects of cadaverine on biotin-dependent proteins. 2. Cadaverine caused little overall change in biotinylated MCCA in roots or shoots. 3. In cdh3 roots, total MCCA protein increased without corresponding increase in biotinylated MCCA, consistent with accumulation of apoprotein under biotin limitation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Western blot with streptavidin probe; western blot with MCCA-specific antibody; qRT-PCR
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Biotin limitation may uncouple apoprotein accumulation from active holoenzyme formation
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a downstream biotinylated protein marker, not as the primary metabolic lesion

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: oct1-1 in WS; oct1-2 in Col-0
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cadaverine treatment with or without biotin supplementation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cofactor supporting carboxylase biotinylation and primary metabolism
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: oct1 mutants are cadaverine hypersensitive, and this hypersensitivity is rescued by exogenous biotin
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. oct1 mutants displayed enhanced root-growth sensitivity to cadaverine. 2. Exogenous biotin abolished cadaverine hypersensitivity of oct1 mutants. 3. This places biotin downstream of or parallel to OCT1 in cadaverine response.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; chemical rescue with biotin
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered cation/polyamine or acyl transport homeostasis may affect growth and stress responses
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as a previously characterized cadaverine-hypersensitive mutant; not the primary mechanistic target in this study

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Col-0 background mutants pao4-1 and pao4-2
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cadaverine treatment with or without biotin supplementation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cofactor supporting carboxylase biotinylation and primary metabolism
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: pao4 mutants are cadaverine hypersensitive, and this phenotype is rescued by exogenous biotin
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. pao4 mutants displayed enhanced root-growth sensitivity to cadaverine. 2. Biotin supplementation suppressed the cadaverine hypersensitivity of pao4 mutants. 3. These results suggest PAO4 influences cadaverine response upstream of or parallel to biotin-related processes.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; chemical rescue with biotin
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changing polyamine catabolism can perturb primary metabolism and reactive oxygen/hydrogen peroxide balance
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Presented mainly as a supporting mutant linking polyamine homeostasis with the biotin-dependent cadaverine response

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Bifunctional biotin biosynthesis enzyme containing BIO1 aminotransferase/DAPA synthase domain and BIO3 dethiobiotin synthetase domain; PLP-dependent enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream bifunctional step; BIO1 converts KAPA to DAPA and BIO3 converts DAPA onward to dethiobiotin
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: BIO1 aminotransferase activity converts KAPA to DAPA, which is channeled to the BIO3 active site for conversion to dethiobiotin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cofactor required for biotinylation of carboxylases involved in fatty acid metabolism, amino acid metabolism, and central metabolism
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms essential intermediate(s) leading directly to biotin synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control point targeted by cadaverine in this study
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: No significant change with cadaverine treatment in whole seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: No significant change with cadaverine treatment in whole seedlings; overexpression in rescue lines confers cadaverine resistance
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Exogenous cadaverine treatment (typically 200 µM on seedlings; 50 µM in vitro enzyme assay); exogenous biotin, DAPA, or KAPA supplementation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes biotin synthesis; cdh3 A662T mutation causes cadaverine hypersensitive primary root growth inhibition, while increased BIO3-BIO1 expression confers cadaverine resistance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Forward genetic screening identified cdh3 as a cadaverine-hypersensitive mutant carrying an A662T missense mutation in BIO3-BIO1. 2. Native-promoter BIO3-BIO1 transgene rescued cdh3 root-growth defects and higher BIO3-BIO1 expression increased cadaverine resistance. 3. Biotin and DAPA, but not KAPA, suppressed cadaverine hypersensitivity, placing cadaverine action at or around BIO1 function. 4. In vitro recombinant enzyme assays showed cadaverine reduces DTB formation, supporting direct inhibition of BIO3-BIO1 activity. 5. Cadaverine did not significantly alter BIO3-BIO1 transcript or protein abundance, indicating enzymatic inhibition rather than expression control.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EMS forward genetic screen; mapping-by-sequencing; transgenic complementation; qRT-PCR; western blot; chemical rescue with biotin/DAPA/KAPA; recombinant in vitro enzyme assay measuring DTB production; root-growth phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Whole-genome next-generation sequencing of pooled F2 mutants; lipidomics by tandem mass spectrometry; accession number not provided
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: BIO3-BIO1 native promoter (genomic rescue construct); endogenous promoter, likely constitutive/native
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Increasing BIO3-BIO1 dosage or engineering cadaverine-insensitive enzyme variants could help sustain biotin synthesis, fatty acid metabolism, and root growth under cadaverine-rich or stress-associated conditions
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Biotin pathway perturbation reduces BCCP1 biotinylation and TAG accumulation; altered root growth and possible cell death in elongation zone under cadaverine exposure
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: cdh3 mutation changes Ala662 to Thr near the PLP cofactor binding site in the BIO1 catalytic pocket; recombinant mutant protein could not be produced successfully, suggesting reduced protein stability
